Unpacking the basic example

Running through the introductory greta example

This introductory example was found on the front page of the greta website.

library(greta)
## 
## Attaching package: 'greta'
## The following objects are masked from 'package:stats':
## 
##     binomial, poisson
## The following objects are masked from 'package:base':
## 
##     %*%, backsolve, beta, colMeans, colSums, diag, forwardsolve,
##     gamma, rowMeans, rowSums, sweep
x <- iris$Petal.Length
y <- iris$Sepal.Length

int <- normal(0, 5)
coef <- normal(0, 3)
sd <- lognormal(0, 3)

mean <- int + coef * x
distribution(y) <- normal(mean, sd)

m <- model(int, coef, sd)

# Throws error for chains argument, removed chains = 3 & reported issue on github. 
draws <- greta::mcmc(m, n_samples = 1000) 

bayesplot::mcmc_trace(draws)

Now to unpack each element

First, as always, we start with data

In this case, we’re interested in if these data suggest there is an association between Petal.Length and Sepal.Length in the canonical iris dataset.

library(greta)

x <- iris$Petal.Length
y <- iris$Sepal.Length

These are two continuous variables.

library(tidyverse) # Sorry, Nick. 

iris %>%
  select(Sepal.Length, Petal.Length) %>% 
  gather(key = "variable",
         value = "Length") %>% 
  ggplot(aes(Length)) +
  geom_histogram() +
  facet_wrap(~ variable)

This is a simple linear model, one that we used ordinary least squares to solve in undergrad.

So, for the \(i\)th observation, we have \[ Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_i + \varepsilon_i \] where \(\varepsilon_i \sim N(0, \sigma^2)\). Which is to say, \[ E(Y) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X. \]
